This sample article is designed to be long enough for realistic scrolling tests while combining narrative notes, code snippets, and media.

Lab Objective

We built a disposable reverse-proxy lab to rehearse request-smuggling and header-confusion scenarios under controlled conditions.
The setup used a front proxy, an app gateway, and a backend service with intentionally uneven parser behavior.

Proxy Map

client -> edge proxy :443
edge proxy -> gateway :8080
gateway -> app service :9000
app service -> datastore :5432

NGINX Edge Snippet

server {
    listen 443 ssl http2;
    server_name lab.local;

    ssl_certificate     /etc/ssl/certs/lab.crt;
    ssl_certificate_key /etc/ssl/private/lab.key;

    location / {
        proxy_http_version 1.1;
        proxy_set_header Connection "";
        proxy_set_header Host $host;
        proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-For $proxy_add_x_forwarded_for;
        proxy_pass http://gateway:8080;
    }
}

Gateway Middleware (Node.js)

import express from "express";
import crypto from "crypto";

const app = express();

app.use((req, res, next) => {
  const traceId = crypto.randomUUID();
  req.traceId = traceId;
  res.setHeader("x-trace-id", traceId);
  next();
});

app.get("/healthz", (_req, res) => {
  res.json({ ok: true, ts: new Date().toISOString() });
});

app.listen(8080, () => {
  console.log("gateway listening on :8080");
});

Test Payload Journal

We tracked payload classes and outcome deltas across parser combinations:

  1. Conflicting Content-Length headers.
  2. Transfer-Encoding ambiguity with chunk boundaries.
  3. Header casing collisions in duplicate key paths.
  4. Hop-by-hop header leakage.
  5. Upstream timeout race conditions.

Structured Findings Example

{
  "test_id": "smuggle-014",
  "vector": "te-cl",
  "frontend_behavior": "accept",
  "gateway_behavior": "accept",
  "backend_behavior": "split-request",
  "impact": "desync window",
  "confidence": "high",
  "mitigation": "strict transfer-encoding policy + duplicate header rejection"
}

Long-form Debrief

The debrief section is intentionally verbose for layout validation.
We observed that parser mismatch issues are rarely visible during ordinary request patterns; they appear during malformed edge cases where intermediate devices disagree on request boundaries.

In operational terms, the biggest win came from harmonizing parser behavior and rejecting ambiguous requests as early as possible.
This reduced exploitability and made logs significantly easier to reason about because request identity remained stable end-to-end.

Another key lesson: replay tooling should record raw bytes, not only parsed fields.
When debugging desynchronization, byte-level reconstruction is often the only trustworthy path.

Finally, we treat this lab journal as living documentation.
As components change versions, old assumptions expire; reproducible notes keep the team aligned and prevent regressions from quietly re-entering the stack.

Quick Utility Script (PowerShell)

$targets = @(
  "https://lab.local/healthz",
  "https://lab.local/"
)

foreach ($url in $targets) {
  try {
    $resp = Invoke-WebRequest -Uri $url -Method GET -UseBasicParsing
    "{0} -> {1}" -f $url, $resp.StatusCode
  } catch {
    "{0} -> ERROR: {1}" -f $url, $_.Exception.Message
  }
}

This closes out the sample post set.
Use these entries to validate feed playback timing, message rendering, image behavior, and code block readability in your IRC-styled theme.
